full_response:
Let  

Event A : “you finish getting into the driver’s seat (door closes, ignition on)”,  
Event B : “the car begins to move away”.

In the inertial frame S in which the measurement is originally made

Δt = t_B – t_A  > 0 , Δx = x_B – x_A ≈ 0                                             (1)

(the car is essentially at the same place for the two events).

1.  Invariant space-time separation  
   The squared interval between the two events is  

   s² = –c²(Δt)² + (Δx)² .                                                            (2)

   Because |Δx| ≪ cΔt, we have s² < 0 : the events are timelike separated.

2.  Lorentz transformation of the time difference  
   For a boost with velocity v along x,

   Δt′ = γ (Δt – v Δx / c²).                                                          (3)

   Since Δx ≈ 0,  
   Δt′ = γ Δt  >  0 for every real γ ≥ 1.  
   Hence the sign of the time difference is unchanged in every inertial frame.

3.  Physical meaning  
   Timelike separation implies that a causal signal (even travelling slower than light) could go from A to B.  
   Special relativity guarantees that the temporal order of timelike-separated events is invariant; only spacelike-separated events can have their order reversed by changing frames.

Therefore all inertial observers must agree that Event A (getting into the car) occurs before Event B (driving away).

\boxed{\text{Yes.  Because the two events are timelike separated, their temporal order is invariant; every inertial frame agrees that you got into the car before you drove away.}}

Confidence: 83%
extracted_answer: \text{Yes.  Because the two events are timelike separated, their temporal order is invariant; every inertial frame agrees that you got into the car before you drove away.}
